patient.chief_complaint: increased leg swelling and shortness of breath

records.content: 75 y/o male with HFrEF (EF 35%, ischemic cardiomyopathy). NYHA Class II. On lisinopril, metoprolol, furosemide. Tolerating medications well. No orthopnea, no PND. Mild bilateral ankle edema. Weight 198 lbs. Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 64, RR 16. JVP not elevated. Lungs: clear. Assessment: Stable HFrEF. Plan: Continue current regimen. Consider adding spironolactone for mortality benefit — pending potassium and renal function check.

75 y/o male with HFrEF. Potassium 4.4, eGFR 58. Appropriate to start spironolactone. Plan: Start spironolactone 25mg daily. Recheck potassium in 1 week, then at 1 month. Continue lisinopril 20mg, metoprolol 100mg, furosemide 40mg. WARNING: Spironolactone + lisinopril = hyperkalemia risk. Must monitor potassium closely.

records.summary: Potassium check — 1 week after spironolactone start
Potassium check — 1 month after spironolactone start

records.content: 75 y/o male with HFrEF on optimal medical therapy (lisinopril, metoprolol, furosemide, spironolactone). NYHA Class II. Weight stable at 196 lbs. Minimal ankle edema. No dyspnea at rest. Assessment: Stable HF. Plan: Continue all medications. Labs in 3 months.

75 y/o male with HFrEF, HTN, T2DM. HbA1c 7.4%. Diabetes management stable. Reports mild back pain x 2 weeks after gardening. Vitals: BP 122/74, HR 62, weight 198 lbs. Assessment: T2DM suboptimal. Back pain — likely musculoskeletal strain. Plan: Continue diabetes meds. Acetaminophen 500mg TID PRN for back pain. Follow up in 3 months.

76 y/o male. Chronic conditions stable. Back pain resolved. Reports occasional dizziness when standing quickly. BP 110/68 sitting, 98/60 standing (orthostatic drop). Assessment: Mild orthostatic hypotension — likely from multiple antihypertensives + diuretic. Plan: Increase fluid intake. Rise slowly. Monitor.

76 y/o male with HFrEF. Reports increased dyspnea on exertion over past month. Can walk 1 block before dyspnea (previously 3-4 blocks). Two-pillow orthopnea. Weight up to 204 lbs (+6 lbs). Bilateral pitting edema 2+. JVP mildly elevated. Lungs: bibasilar crackles. Assessment: HF exacerbation, NYHA Class III. Plan: Increase furosemide to 80mg daily. Strict fluid restriction 1.5L/day. Daily weights. Recheck in 1 week. Consider digoxin if not improving.

76 y/o male. Weight down to 200 lbs (-4 lbs). Dyspnea slightly improved. Edema reduced to 1+. Adding digoxin 0.125mg daily for additional symptom control. Plan: Continue furosemide 80mg, spironolactone 25mg, lisinopril 20mg, metoprolol 100mg. Digoxin 0.125mg daily. Recheck potassium and digoxin level in 2 weeks.

76 y/o male with HFrEF. Currently NYHA Class II-III. On multiple medications. Reports occasional nausea and reduced appetite. Weight 196 lbs. Vitals: BP 108/66, HR 58. Assessment: Multiple concerns — low HR (on metoprolol + digoxin), borderline BP, nausea could be digoxin toxicity vs GI. Plan: Check digoxin level, potassium, renal function. If digoxin level elevated, contact cardiology.

76 y/o male with HFrEF. ONGOING CONCERN: Potassium has been consistently elevated for over a year: 5.0 → 5.1 → 5.2 → 5.4 → 5.3 → 5.5. Patient is on THREE medications that raise potassium: lisinopril (ACE inhibitor), spironolactone (potassium-sparing diuretic), and declining renal function further impairs potassium excretion. Additionally, furosemide dose was increased which can cause hypomagnesemia — magnesium is now LOW at 1.5, which worsens cardiac arrhythmia risk alongside hyperkalemia. Digoxin level 1.8 — at upper limit. Hyperkalemia + hypomagnesemia increases digoxin toxicity risk. Assessment: Dangerous electrolyte situation. Plan: Reduce spironolactone to 12.5mg daily. Start magnesium oxide 400mg BID. Recheck potassium in 3 days. Consider holding digoxin if potassium doesn't improve. Low potassium diet counseling.

ground_truth_issues.description: Persistent hyperkalemia (potassium consistently 5.0-5.5 mEq/L over 12+ months) in a patient on spironolactone + lisinopril + declining renal function. The hyperkalemia was not addressed promptly — potassium was elevated on multiple consecutive labs (records #6, #8, #10, #13, #16, #18) but spironolactone dose was not reduced until potassium reached 5.5 (record #19). Adding digoxin to a patient with chronic hyperkalemia further increased cardiac arrhythmia risk, as hyperkalemia potentiates digoxin toxicity.
ground_truth_issues.clinical_reference: Spironolactone + ACE inhibitor combination requires potassium monitoring within 1 week, then monthly. Potassium > 5.0 on consecutive labs should trigger dose reduction or discontinuation of potassium-elevating agents. Guidelines recommend holding spironolactone if K+ > 5.5 or reducing dose if K+ > 5.0 persistently.
ground_truth_issues.what_should_have_happened: When potassium first exceeded 5.0 consistently (records #6-8, March-June 2022), spironolactone should have been reduced or dietary counseling intensified. The combination of spironolactone + lisinopril + declining eGFR is a well-known high-risk scenario for hyperkalemia. Adding digoxin without first addressing the hyperkalemia was additionally risky.
